result.markdown:
--- title: "Data Visualization I & II" subtitle: "POL 051 - Summer Session 1 2026" author: "Haley Daarstad" footer: "[haleydaarstad.com/pol051-su26/](http://haleydaarstad.com/pol051-su26/)" logo: "images/logo.png" format: revealjs: slide-number: true height: 900 width: 1600 code-line-numbers: true smooth-scrawl: true theme: [simple, html/custom.scss] incremental: true title-slide-attributes: data-background-image: img/dubois-spiral-2.png data-background-size: contain data-background-position: right data-background-color: "#dc354a" --- ## Why visualize data? ::: incremental - Data carries **weight** in our society - **Visualizing** data is an effective way to convey information, convince, argue - Visualization can be used to tell **The Truth™️** (or not) ::: ## Dataviz to inform ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/nyt-good.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Dataviz to mislead ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/gun-bad.png){fig-align='center' width=40%} ::: ::: ## Inform? or mislead? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: # Making graphs in `R` {background-color="#dc354a"} ## The Gapminder dataset ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap
Afghanistan Asia 1952 29 8425333 779
Afghanistan Asia 1957 30 9240934 821
Afghanistan Asia 1962 32 10267083 853
Afghanistan Asia 1967 34 11537966 836
Afghanistan Asia 1972 36 13079460 740
````` ::: ::: ## Rows are observations ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap
Afghanistan Asia 1952 29 8425333 779
Afghanistan Asia 1957 30 9240934 821
Afghanistan Asia 1962 32 10267083 853
Afghanistan Asia 1967 34 11537966 836
Afghanistan Asia 1972 36 13079460 740
````` ::: ::: In a dataset, **rows** are **observations** . . . The data we **observe** for *Afghanistan* in the year *1952* ## Rows are observations ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
id age degree race sex
1 47 Bachelor White Male
2 61 High School White Male
3 72 Bachelor White Male
4 43 High School White Female
5 55 Graduate White Female
````` ::: ::: In **survey** data, an observation is typically a **person** who took the survey (a *respondent*) ## Columns are variables ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap
Afghanistan Asia 1952 29 8425333 779
Afghanistan Asia 1957 30 9240934 821
Afghanistan Asia 1962 32 10267083 853
Afghanistan Asia 1967 34 11537966 836
Afghanistan Asia 1972 36 13079460 740
````` ::: ::: In a dataset, **columns** are **variables** . . . Life expectancy and GDP per capita are some of the **variables** in our data ## The final graph ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: There are four variables on this graph. What are they? ## The **grammar** of graphics {.center} . . . Graphs have an internal logic, or **grammar** that connects data to visuals . . . **Data** = variables in a dataset . . . **Aesthetic** = visual property of a graph (position, shape, color, etc.) . . . **Geometry** = representation of an aesthetic (point, line, text, etc.) ## Mapping data to aesthetics ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:---------------|:-----------------|:--------| |GDP per capita |Position(x-axis) |Point | |Life expectancy |Position (y-axis) |Point | |Continent |Color |Point | |Population |Size |Point | ::: ::: 1. Take the **data**, 2. map it onto an **aesthetic**, 3. and visualize it with a **geometry** ## In R ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |Data |aes() |geom_ | |:---------|:-----|:------------| |gdpPercap |x |geom_point() | |lifeExp |y |geom_point() | |continent |color |geom_point() | |pop |size |geom_point() | ::: ::: Use the variable names *exactly* as they appear in the data, map them onto the *exact* function names in R ## `ggplot()`: our first function 😢 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="1"} ggplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: don't fret; You will not memorize these as they appear on screen ## `ggplot`: specify the data ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Use `aes()` to map variables to aesthetics ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## add geometries and layers using `+` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## mapping population to size in `aes()` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## mapping continent to color in `aes()` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## Other layers: add the missing titles with `labs()` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent)) + geom_point() + labs(x = "GDP per capita", y = "Life expectancy", title = "Global wealth and health in 2007", size = "Population", color = "") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){fig-align='center' width=70%} ::: ::: Notice that text is placed within **quotation marks**! ## Other layers: add a theme ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent)) + geom_point() + labs(x = "GDP per capita", y = "Life expectancy", title = "Global wealth and health in 2007") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: There are many more themes, [here are a few](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/ggtheme.html) ## The final formula ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent)) + geom_point() + labs(x = "GDP per capita", y = "Life expectancy", title = "Global wealth and health in 2007") + theme_bw() ``` ::: 1. Tell `ggplot()` the data we want to plot 2. Map all variables onto aesthetics within `aes()` 3. Add layers like `geom_point()` and `theme_bw()` using `+` ## What's that country way out on the bottom right? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: try labeling the points 🚨 1. Add labels to each point by mapping country names onto the `label` aesthetic within `aes()` 2. Add `geom_text` layer to your plot to plot the names ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("gapminder") library(gapminder) gap_07 <- gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) ``` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## The basic plot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){fig-align='center' width=70%} ::: ::: ## Map country names to label aesthetic ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent, label = country)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){fig-align='center' width=70%} ::: ::: ## Plot the labels ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = continent, label = country)) + geom_point() + geom_text() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){fig-align='center' width=70%} ::: ::: ## What did we do? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Data Aesthetic Geometry
gdpPercap x geom_point()
lifeExp y geom_point()
continent color geom_point()
pop size geom_point()
country label geom_text()
````` ::: ::: Take your *data*, map it onto an *aesthetic*, represent with a *geometry* ## 🇺🇸 The presidents 🇺🇸 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} elections_historic %>% select(year, winner, win_party, ec_pct, popular_pct, two_term) %>% slice_head(n = 4) %>% knitr::kable(caption = "Sample of presidential elections", digits = 2) ``` ::: ## 🚨 Your turn 🚨 1. Make a plot of presidential election results using the `elections_historic` dataset 2. \% of popular vote (x-axis, `popular_pct`) and % of electoral college vote (y-axis, `ec_pct`) 3. map the winner's party to the `color` aesthetic, whether or not president served two terms to `shape`, and add labels to each point (use `winner_label`) ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("socviz") library(socviz) ``` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## US Presidents ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-30-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: What's going on here? ## What did we do? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:------------|:---------|:------------| |popular_pct |x |geom_point() | |ec_pct |y |geom_point() | |win_party |color |geom_point() | |two_term |shape |geom_point() | |winner_label |label |geom_text() | ::: ::: Take your *data*, map it onto an *aesthetic*, represent with a *geometry* # A graph for every season {.center} - There are many graphs out there - Each one works best in a specific **context** - Each one combines different **aesthetics** and **geometries** # Key plotting questions {.center} - What am I trying to **show**? - (a distribution, a relationship, a comparison, an amount) - What **kind** of variables do I have? - (continuous, discrete, something in between) - What **aesthetics** and **geometries** do I need for this plot? - (x-axis, y-axis, color, size, shape, etc.) ## What kind of variable do I have? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
General Social Survey
id age degree race num_kids
1 47 Bachelor White 3
2 61 High School White 0
3 72 Bachelor White 2
4 43 High School White 4
5 55 Graduate White 2
````` ::: ::: ::: incremental - **Continuous** variables take on lots of values (GDP, population, income, age, etc.) ::: ## What kind of variable do I have? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
General Social Survey
id age degree race num_kids
1 47 Bachelor White 3
2 61 High School White 0
3 72 Bachelor White 2
4 43 High School White 4
5 55 Graduate White 2
````` ::: ::: ::: incremental - *Discrete* or **categorical** variables takes on a few values, often "qualitative" (yes/no, 1/0, race, etc.) ::: ## Graph 1 - The Scatterplot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-34-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: . . . The **scatterplot** visualizes the relationship between two **continuous** variables . . . Shows every point in the data, reveals trends and *outliers* ## The grammar of scatterplots ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: The data | gdpPercap| lifeExp| |---------:|-------:| | 974.58| 43.83| | 5937.03| 76.42| | 6223.37| 72.30| | 4797.23| 42.73| | 12779.38| 75.32| ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Mapping the data |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:---------|:---------|:------------| |gdpPercap |x |geom_point() | |lifeExp |y |geom_point() | ::: ::: ::: :::: :::::::
. . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: Typically, we put the **cause** on the x-axis and the **effect** on the y-axis ## Scatterplots are for *continuous* variables ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-38-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: Plot is uninformative because continent is *discrete* (i.e., a category) ## Graph 2: the time series ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-39-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: The **time series** uses a *line* to show you how a variable (y-axis) moves over time (x-axis) ## The grammar of time series ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: The data | year| avg_yrs| |----:|-------:| | 1952| 49.06| | 1957| 51.51| | 1962| 53.61| | 1967| 55.68| | 1972| 57.65| ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Mapping the data |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:-------|:---------|:-----------| |year |x |geom_line() | |avg_yrs |y |geom_line() | ::: ::: ::: :::: :::::::
. . . Notice the new geometry, `geom_line()` ## The time series ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(life_yr, aes(x = year, y = avg_yrs)) + geom_line() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-42-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: 💸Recession💸 🚨 - Look at the `economics` dataset from the `tidyverse` package - Type and run `economics` to see the data - Type and run `?economics` to read about the data - Make a time series of unemployment over time - Can you identify the **recessions**? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## 💸Recession💸 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-44-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Multiple time series Sometimes we observe multiple **units** over time; how can we visualize these? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |country |continent | year| lifeExp| pop| gdpPercap| |:-------------|:---------|----:|-------:|----------:|----------:| |Bolivia |Americas | 1987| 57.251| 6156369| 2753.6915| |United States |Americas | 1977| 73.380| 220239000| 24072.6321| |Ghana |Africa | 1992| 57.501| 16278738| 925.0602| |China |Asia | 1997| 70.426| 1230075000| 2289.2341| |United States |Americas | 1997| 76.810| 272911760| 35767.4330| ::: ::: ## Start from scratch ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gapminder_sample) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-46-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Add aesthetics ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gapminder_sample, aes(x = year, y = lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-47-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Add geometry: 🤢 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gapminder_sample, aes(x = year, y = lifeExp)) + geom_line() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-48-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## using `color` to separate lines ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gapminder_sample, aes(x = year, y = lifeExp, color = country)) + geom_line() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-49-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## Multiple time series ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data = gapminder_sample, aes(x = year, y = lifeExp, color = country)) + geom_line() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-50-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: . . . These are useful for *comparing* **trends** across units (countries, places, people, etc) ## Graph 3: the histogram ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-51-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: A **histogram** shows you how a continuous variable is *distributed* ## Interpreting histograms ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-52-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: notes What do we see here? ::: ## The grammar of histograms ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: The data | lifeExp| |-------:| | 43.83| | 76.42| | 72.30| | 42.73| | 75.32| ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Mapping the data |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:-------|:---------|:----------------| |lifeExp |x |geom_histogram() | ::: ::: ::: :::: :::::::
. . . Notice the new geometry, `geom_histogram()`; and that a histogram *only* uses the x-axis! ## The histogram ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gap_07, aes(x = lifeExp)) + geom_histogram() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-55-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: organs 🫁🧠 🚨 ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} In some countries, when you die it is **assumed** you want to donate your organs - To not donate, you have to **opt out** - In other countries, when you die it is **assumed** you *do not* want to donate your organs - To not donate, you have to **opt in** ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Sample from organdata
country donors opt
Finland 17.1 NA
Denmark 12.9 In
Austria 25.9 Out
Austria 23.9 Out
Italy 10.1 In
````` ::: ::: ::: ::::: ## 🚨 Your turn: organs 🫁🧠 🚨 Using the `organdata` dataset: 1. Make a histogram of country's organ donation rate (`donors`) 2. Then set the fill aesthetic to `opt`, whether donors have to opt in or opt out of donating. How does the graph change? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## organs 🫁🧠 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-58-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Graph 4: the barplot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-59-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: . . . Barplots place a *category* (place, country, person, etc) on one axis and a *quantity* (amount, average, median, etc.) on another . . . Useful for making **comparisons**, highlighting **differences** ::: notes Why would there be differences across TV consumption? ::: ## The grammar of barplots ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: The data |marital | tv| |:-------------|----:| |No answer | 2.56| |Never married | 3.11| |Separated | 3.55| |Divorced | 3.09| |Widowed | 3.91| ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Mapping the data |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:-------|:---------|:----------| |tv |x |geom_col() | |marital |y |geom_col() | ::: ::: ::: :::: :::::::
. . . ::: callout-note You could switch the x and y mapping around, but I think categories look better on the y-axis ::: ## The barplot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(tv, aes(y = marital, x = tv)) + geom_col() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-62-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Graph 5: the boxplot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-63-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: . . . Boxplots compare **distributions** of *continuous* variables across groups ::: notes they show us *more* than the barplot which only gives us a quantity for each group ::: ## Compare distributions: the boxplot ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} Boxplots contain a lot of info 🥵: - bold line is the **median** observation - box is the **middle** 50% of observations - thin lines show you min and max value, *except...* - the dots, which are **outlier** observations ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-64-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: ::::: ::: notes interpret ::: ## The grammar of boxplots ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: The data |continent | lifeExp| |:---------|-------:| |Asia | 43.83| |Europe | 76.42| |Africa | 72.30| |Africa | 42.73| |Americas | 75.32| ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Mapping the data |Data |Aesthetic |Geometry | |:--------|:---------|:--------------| |contient |y |geom_boxplot() | |lifeExp |x |geom_boxplot() | ::: ::: ::: :::: :::::::
. . . ::: callout-note You could switch the x and y mapping around, but I think categories look better on the y-axis ::: ## The boxplot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(y = continent, x = lifeExp)) + geom_boxplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-67-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## The five(-ish) graphs ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Graph aes() geom_ Purpose
Scatterplot x = cause, y = effect point() Relationships
Time series x = date, y = variable line() Trends
Histogram x = cont. variable histogram() Distributions
Barplot y = category, x = quantity col() Compare amounts
Boxplot y = category, x = cont. variable boxplot() Compare distributions
````` ::: ::: . . . Know how and when to use which! # Making better graphs {background-color="#dc354a"} - We've barely scratched the surface; there's many more aesthetics, geometries, and layers in `ggplot()` - Here are some of my favorite ones - And some ideas for making graphs better ## Showing "movement" using panels ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-69-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: . . . We can use **panels** to show *movement* of a variable across time, space, etc. ::: notes What is this graph showing us? ::: ## Using facet_wrap ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = lifeExp)) + geom_histogram() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-70-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Using facet_wrap ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = lifeExp)) + geom_histogram() + facet_wrap(vars(year)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-71-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ::: callout-note Make sure the facetting variable is wrapped in `vars()`! ::: ## Make aesthetics static ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gap_07, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point(size = 4, color = "orange", shape = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-72-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: Take your aesthetics out of `aes()` and into `geom()` to make them *static* ## Ridge plots (better than grouped histograms) ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggridges) ggplot(gapminder, aes(y = continent, x = lifeExp)) + geom_density_ridges() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-73-1.png){fig-align='center' width=70%} ::: ::: Ease visual comparison + kinda looks like the Joy Division album ## Beeswarm plots (alternative boxplots) ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggbeeswarm) ggplot(gapminder, aes(y = continent, x = lifeExp)) + geom_quasirandom() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-74-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: Beeswarm plots tell us something boxplots don't: the number observations by group; used recently by the [NYT](https://www.nytimes.com/2022/09/11/nyregion/hasidic-yeshivas-schools-new-york.html) ## Use different color and fill scales ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = lifeExp, fill = continent)) + geom_histogram() + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-75-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: `scale_fill_brewer()` for `fill`, `scale_color_brewer` for `color` ## My favorite scale (right now) ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = lifeExp, fill = continent)) + geom_histogram() + scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "magma") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-76-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: `scale_fill_viridis_d` for *discrete* variables, `scale_fill_viridis_d` for continuous ## Many other themes ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-77-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: `theme_spongeBob()` from `tvthemes` package, many more online

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
